research_answer: Copper poses a methodological challenge for measuring Chile's export sophistication because it is both highly dominant in Chile's export mix and relatively unsophisticated as a product. The EXPY metric — which weights each product's sophistication by its share in a country's export basket — can be pulled downward by copper's dominant share even if Chile's non-copper exports were actually quite sophisticated. In other words, copper's large weight in the total can mask the quality of the rest of the basket. However, even when analysts strip out copper and look only at non-copper exports, Chile's export sophistication is still falling behind — surpassed by Turkey (which started 1975 at half Chile's non-copper EXPY level) and now only two-thirds the level of Hungary. So copper distortion alone cannot explain Chile's sophistication problem; the non-copper export basket is itself genuinely unsophisticated.
supporting_quotes: Chile's export basket is dominated by copper exports: A relatively unsophisticated product. If Chile's non-copper exports were highly sophisticated, this could be hidden behind copper's dominant share in total exports, making EXPY deceivingly small.

It has been surpassed by Turkey, which began 1975 with an EXPY barely half of that of Chile (non-copper). Chile's non-copper export basket had the same level of export sophistication as Hungary in 1975, but as of 2004 was barely more than 2/3rds that of Hungary.
